turns.text: Hello Dian, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the early stages of pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s a lot to process, honestly. I think I’m still waiting for it to feel entirely real, even though I’m ten weeks in and the morning sickness has been... well, let’s just say it’s definitely here. 

Being in this by myself—well, I’m independent, I’ve always been—but when you’re staring at the ceiling at 3:00 AM in a quiet apartment, "independent" just starts to feel a lot like "lonely." I want to do everything right. I’ve been researching the Tdap booster and the flu shots because I’m terrified of missing even a small detail that could protect the baby. I guess I’m trying to be the "perfect" patient, but that’s just a way to keep the panic at bay. 

When I saw that flickering heartbeat on the monitor during my last visit, the clinical stuff just fell away. I wasn't just a low-risk chart to be checked off; I was someone’s mom. That’s the part I’m trying to hold onto, even when the doctor talks about me like I’m just a routine case. I need them to know that while my blood pressure is fine and I'm healthy, the emotional weight of this is real. I’m just trying to figure out how to be both the mother I want to be and the person I still have to be, all at once.
Tell me about the time when you first started thinking seriously about having a baby — what was going on in your life, and what did that feel like?

To be honest, it wasn’t some grand plan I’d been mapping out for years. At twenty-seven, I think I was mostly focused on trying to find more stable hours at work and just keeping my head above water here in Fall River. It’s hard enough to feel like you’ve got your own life figured out, let alone someone else’s.

When I realized it was actually happening, the first feeling wasn't exactly joy—it was pure, unfiltered terror. I looked around my apartment, just seeing the silence of it, and the reality hit me that I’d be the one making every call. No partner to turn to, no one to share that late-night panic with. I spent a lot of time just walking down by the water, trying to reconcile the person I was—the one who prides herself on being self-sufficient—with this sudden, overwhelming vulnerability. 

But then, that shift happened. It wasn’t a lightning bolt, but more like a quiet realization that I had this capacity for strength I hadn't tapped into yet. It felt like I was standing on the edge of a cliff, terrified of the drop, but also knowing that I was going to jump because there was no other choice. It’s a strange, heavy, beautiful feeling—carrying all this hope and all this worry in one body.
Tell me about how you went about preparing — or thinking about preparing — for pregnancy. What steps, if any, did you take, and what guided those choices?

It’s funny, because my version of "preparing" feels like I’m trying to study for an exam I’m not sure I’m ready to take. I’m a total planner by nature—maybe because I have to be, since I’m doing this solo. I didn't have someone to talk through prenatal vitamins or doctor choices with, so I turned to my laptop. I spent hours reading about everything from the Tdap booster to the right kind of diet. I think I just wanted to feel like I had some control over a situation that feels like it’s changing my body by the minute.

I guess the main thing guiding me is just wanting to be a "perfect" patient. I’m so worried that if I miss a detail or don’t ask the right question, I’ll be failing the baby before they’re even born. At my last appointment, I had this whole list of questions about vaccines and schedules, and I’m sure the midwife thought I was a bit intense. But for me, it’s not just about the medical data. It’s about replacing that fear of the unknown with something tangible. If I can tick every box, maybe I can convince myself that I’m actually capable of doing this, even when the apartment feels too quiet.
Tell me about any conversations you had with a GP, midwife, or other health professional before you became pregnant. What was that like?

To be honest, I didn’t have those conversations. That’s probably part of why I feel so behind, even though I’m doing all this reading now. I didn’t go in for a "preconception visit" because I wasn't really planning on this, and honestly, the thought of walking into a doctor's office to talk about a hypothetical pregnancy felt a bit… premature, I guess? Or maybe just daunting.

When I finally did go in, it was because I was already pregnant. My first visit felt very fast. They checked my vitals, took my history, and because I’m "low-risk," the whole thing felt very routine—like I was just another line item in their afternoon. It was efficient, I suppose, but I left feeling like I had a dozen questions still trapped in my throat. I didn't feel like I could ask them about the anxiety or the loneliness, because that’s not really on their checklists, is it?

I wish there had been more space in those initial appointments to just be a person, not a patient. I think I’m still trying to find a way to bridge that gap—to make them see that even if my charts look "perfectly normal," the person carrying the chart is trying to navigate a whole lot of life on her own.
Tell me about what it was like to try to conceive — however long or short that period was for you. What stands out most from that time?

I think the most honest answer is that I didn't "try" in the way people usually mean when they talk about conception. There weren't months of tracking cycles or taking ovulation tests or holding my breath every time my period was a day late. It happened, and suddenly, the path of my life just... swerved.

If I look back at that time—the very beginning—what stands out isn't a medical process or a journey of preparation. It was the absolute, jarring silence of the moment I realized it was real. One minute, I was just Dian, heading to work, dealing with the daily grind on South Main, and the next minute, the entire trajectory of my future had shifted. 

It was a strange, disorienting sort of whiplash. Because it wasn't a "planned" journey, I skipped the phase of excited anticipation. Instead, I went straight from "this is my life" to "I am responsible for another life." That’s a heavy weight to wake up with. I think that's why I'm so obsessed with the clinical details now—the vaccines, the symptoms, the data. It’s my way of trying to catch up, to gain some sort of footing in a reality that felt like it hit me all at once.
How did this period affect your relationship with your partner, or with those close to you?

That’s a complicated question for me. Since I’m doing this on my own, there wasn't a partner there to share the news with, to hold my hand, or to help me process the suddenness of it all. It’s just me. And honestly, that’s where the hardest part of this journey has been so far. 

I haven't told many people yet. There’s a certain vulnerability in sharing this when you’re not where society says you should be—unmarried, doing it solo, and still trying to figure out my own career path. I feel this need to have my "act together" before I let people in, because I don't want the pity, and I definitely don't want the questions I don't have the answers to yet. 

My friends are supportive, sure, but they’re in different places. They can't truly understand the specific weight of lying in bed at night, wondering how I'm going to manage the late-night feedings or the daycare costs all by myself. It’s made me retreat into myself a bit more, I think. I find myself leaning on the healthcare system and my research because, as clinical as it is, it’s safer than opening up to people who might look at me with concern instead of just seeing me as a mother. I’m learning to be my own support system, but it’s a lonely kind of practice.
Tell me about the picture you had in your mind of what pregnancy and becoming a parent would be like — where did those ideas come from?

Growing up, I think I just absorbed the usual images—those soft-focus, romanticized versions of motherhood where everything is always bright, clean, and shared with a smiling partner. You see it in movies, or just catch glimpses of it in magazines at the grocery store. I think I subconsciously assumed that if I ever got to this point, there would be a "right" way for it to happen, and that there would be two sets of hands doing the heavy lifting.

But my reality isn't soft-focus. It’s hard edges and fluorescent office lights. The picture in my head now is much more grounded, and frankly, a lot more intimidating. Instead of a partner to discuss names or nursery colors with, my "team" is a midwife who I see for fifteen minutes every few weeks. My ideas now come from the reality of my own apartment—the quiet, the bills, and the sheer logistics of holding down my job while my body changes.

I’ve had to dismantle that old picture entirely. I’m painting a new one now, one where I am the sole anchor. It’s not the one I expected, and it’s certainly not the one society talks about, but I’m realizing that my version of motherhood is going to be forged in the strength I’m finding in these first few months of doing it all on my own.
How much were those ideas shaped by your own family's experiences — your mother's, or others close to you?

My mother’s experience is actually something I’ve been thinking about a lot lately, though I haven’t really said it out loud. She had my siblings and me in a completely different era, and there was always this unspoken expectation that motherhood was something you did *with* a partner, no questions asked. She had a network—grandparents nearby, neighbors who popped in—that made the whole thing look like a community effort.

But my reality feels like the exact opposite. My family is supportive, but they're not exactly in a position to be the "village" I might need. I look at my mom and I see how much she sacrificed, but I also see how much she leaned on my father for the things that kept the house running. Since I don't have that, I’ve had to rewrite the script. I feel like I’m breaking a mold, and that’s both empowering and terrifying. 

I’m constantly wondering if I’m measuring up to some phantom standard she set, but then I remember that she didn't have to navigate being a first-timer in a city where she’s essentially building her own path from scratch. It’s hard to reconcile her story with mine. I’m proud to be doing this, but I do find myself wishing I had her roadmap, even if I know I have to draw my own map anyway.
Tell me about any financial, practical, or life circumstances that were part of the picture as you were thinking about having a baby.

The financial side of things is probably what keeps me up the latest at night. I work full-time, but let’s be real—the cost of living, especially when you’re factoring in everything a baby needs, is not something you can just wish away. I’m constantly doing mental math, toggling between my paycheck and the estimates I find online for diapers, clothes, and childcare. It’s like a puzzle where the pieces don't quite fit, and I’m the only one trying to jam them together.

Then there’s the practical stuff. I live in a decent enough apartment on South Main, but it’s not exactly built for a nursery, and I’m already trying to figure out where a crib would even go. Every time I see a store display for baby gear, my first instinct isn't to look at the cute colors—it’s to look at the price tag and wonder, "How many hours of work does that cost?"

It’s exhausting to have to think about every single cent. I want to give this baby everything, but I’m acutely aware of the limits of a single income. My life is a balance of trying to keep my job performance high while my body is doing this whole "growing a person" thing, and some days, the exhaustion is so heavy that I wonder how I’ll keep both plates spinning. It’s a lot of pressure, but it’s my reality.
Were there things you felt you needed to have 'in place' before starting, and did that match what actually happened?

I honestly thought I needed the whole "package"—a savings account that could weather any storm, a partner who would be there to handle the sleepless nights, and a sense of having my professional life completely settled. I grew up thinking you checked the boxes: finish school, get the stable job, find the right person, then start the family. I imagined a life where everything was aligned and orderly before I ever saw that first positive test.

But life just doesn’t care about your checklist, does it? The reality is that I didn't have any of those things "in place." I’m still working on the savings, I’m doing this journey solo, and my career is still very much a work in progress. It’s been a total crash course in letting go of the idea that I can control the timing. 

At first, I felt like a failure because I hadn’t hit those milestones I thought were mandatory. But now, ten weeks in, I’m realizing that "being ready" is a myth. You just step into the fire and you grow into the person you need to be. It didn't match the plan, not by a long shot, but maybe the plan was just a comfort blanket I didn't actually need. I'm finding that my strength isn't in what I have in my bank account, but in how I'm handling the uncertainty.
Tell me about the moment you found out you were pregnant — and what the weeks that followed were like for you.

The moment I saw those two lines on the test, the world just sort of went quiet. I was sitting on the edge of my bathtub, staring at the stick like it was an alien object. I think I held my breath for a full minute. My first thought wasn't "Oh, how wonderful"—it was just, "Okay, Dian. What do you do now?" It felt like the air had been sucked out of the room.

The weeks that followed were a blur of intense, high-functioning anxiety. I went into "fix-it" mode immediately. I started making lists of everything—when I needed to see a doctor, what foods were safe, what supplements to take. I was terrified of doing something wrong, so I treated the pregnancy like a project. I felt like I was walking on eggshells with my own body, hyper-aware of every cramp and every wave of nausea.

It was lonely. That’s the part that stands out most. I had this massive secret growing inside me, and everywhere I went—the grocery store, the bus, my office—I felt like I was hiding a giant, ticking clock. I’d look at people and wonder if they could tell, if they could see how much I was holding back. I just wanted to be the "perfect" pregnant woman, but inside, I was just a girl who was scared to death.
Tell me about your antenatal care — the appointments, check-ups, and the people who looked after you during your pregnancy. What was that experience like overall?

My appointments have been a strange mix of relief and frustration. I go into that office in Fall River and, for those twenty minutes, I feel like I’m finally doing something concrete to protect this baby. Hearing that heartbeat for the first time? That was the only moment I’ve felt truly connected to the person I’m becoming.

But the care itself… it’s very clinical. I’m a "low-risk" case, which means I’m basically treated like a healthy person who doesn’t need much more than a routine check of my vitals. They’re professional, sure, but it feels like they’re reading from a script. When I bring up the Tdap or ask detailed questions about the post-birth schedule, I can see them glancing at the clock. It feels like they have a box to check, and once the blood pressure and the ultrasound are done, I’m just supposed to go back to my quiet apartment and wait for the next month.

I wish they understood that when you’re doing this alone, every appointment is a lifeline. I’m not just looking for a measurement of the baby’s growth; I’m looking for someone to look at me and acknowledge that this is a huge, scary, life-altering experience. I need them to see past the chart and realize that I’m the one who has to go home and navigate all the "what-ifs" by myself.
Did you see the same midwife or provider regularly, or was it different people each time? How did that feel?

It’s been a mix, which hasn't exactly helped with the anxiety. I’ve seen a couple of different people in the practice, and every time I walk in, I have to re-introduce myself and explain again that, yes, this is my first, and yes, I’m doing this solo. It’s draining to have to reset that connection over and over. You spend the first few minutes just catching them up on your history instead of actually talking about how you’re feeling. 

There’s one midwife I saw recently who felt a little more attentive—she actually paused when I was rambling about the Tdap booster and looked me in the eye—but most of the time, it’s whoever is available on the schedule. It makes the care feel impersonal, like I’m just a placeholder in their day. When you’re in a room alone, the continuity of care really matters. If I could see the same person every time, I think I’d feel more comfortable admitting that I’m struggling with the isolation. With a revolving door of providers, I just stick to the medical questions. I don’t want to be the "needy" patient who takes up too much time. I just want to make sure I’m doing everything right so that when I finally leave the hospital, I’m ready.
Tell me about any screening or testing you were offered or had during your pregnancy — how did you make sense of it all and make decisions about it?

The testing felt like another world I had to learn to navigate. When they offered the different screenings, I felt this wave of pressure—like every decision was a test of whether I was being a "responsible" enough mother. I spent hours reading about each one, trying to weigh the pros and cons while sitting by myself in my living room. I didn't have anyone to bounce these thoughts off of, so I had to be my own sounding board.

I ended up choosing the ones that gave me the most peace of mind, even if they were more invasive or involved more waiting. For me, making sense of it all came down to data. If I could get an answer—even a complicated one—it felt better than the not knowing. The waiting period between the test and getting the results was agonizing; I remember staring at my phone, waiting for a call, feeling like if something was "off," I’d be facing it totally alone.

It’s just... there’s something cold about how those tests are presented. They’re handed to you as a list of options, and you’re expected to make a call based on a brochure and a quick, five-minute chat. I wanted someone to sit down with me and actually say, "Here is why this matters for your specific case." Instead, I had to teach myself. I’m proud I did it, but it shouldn't be that hard.
Tell me about where you turned for information and support during your pregnancy — what sources mattered most to you, and why?

My phone and my laptop are my constant companions now. It sounds silly, but I spend hours on forums and medical websites just digging for answers. When I can’t sleep—which is a lot lately—I’m reading about the Tdap schedule, or what to expect in the second trimester, or even just how other women handle the "solo" aspect of all this. Those online communities actually matter the most to me, because even if I’m just reading, I see other people asking the same questions I’m too scared to ask my midwife.

It’s not perfect, but it’s the only village I have right now. My friends mean well, but they don’t get the clinical anxiety; they just tell me to "relax" and "it’ll be fine," which honestly makes me feel more alone. I don’t want to be told it’ll be fine—I want to know *how* to make sure it’s fine. 

I’ve also started reading books on fetal development. It’s like the more I know about what’s happening inside me, the less out-of-control I feel. It’s my way of being a "good mother" before the baby is even here. If I can’t have someone holding my hand in the office, I’ll at least have the knowledge to advocate for myself and my baby. It’s a lonely comfort, but it’s all I’ve got.
Tell me about how your body changing during pregnancy felt for you — physically and emotionally. How did you relate to those changes?

It’s been the most surreal thing. Some days I look in the mirror and I don’t recognize the person staring back—there’s this slight rounding that just didn’t exist a few weeks ago. Physically, it’s a constant tug-of-war. The morning sickness is exhausting, and the fatigue... it’s not just "tired," it’s like my whole body is being unplugged. 

But emotionally? It’s a strange, bittersweet dance. Every symptom feels like proof that I’m actually doing this, that there’s life inside me. I’ll be sitting at my desk at work, feeling a little cramp, and instead of just worrying, I’ll put my hand over my stomach and try to center myself. It’s the only time I feel like I’m not just "Dian the employee" or "Dian the person who lives alone." I’m someone’s home.

At the same time, I feel a weird sense of betrayal from my own body. It’s doing all this work without asking me, and I’m just trying to keep up. I’m protective of it now, in a way I never was before. I used to be so critical of how I looked, but now, those changes feel sacred. It’s like a countdown—every stretch, every ache is a sign that I’m getting closer to the day I won't be alone in this apartment anymore. It’s frightening, but I’m learning to be kind to myself.
Were there aspects of the physical changes that were harder to come to terms with than others?

The most difficult part is the loss of control over my own energy and my own stamina. I’ve always been someone who prides herself on being able to power through—I’m a hard worker, I’m independent, and I’m used to pushing myself until the job is done. But these last few weeks, my body just says "no." I’ll be in the middle of a shift at work, trying to keep up my usual pace, and suddenly, the nausea or the sheer exhaustion hits, and I have to stop. That vulnerability is really hard for me to sit with.

It feels like my body is prioritizing this tiny, flickering heartbeat over everything else I’m trying to manage, and I have to learn to step back and rest when I’d rather be productive. It’s hard to accept that I can’t "muscle" my way through this pregnancy like I do everything else in my life. Every time I feel that physical limitation, it reminds me that I’m not just responsible for myself anymore. It’s a bit of a grieving process for the version of me who could do it all, I suppose. I’m learning to trade that independence for something… different, I guess. But honestly? Some days I just feel incredibly frustrated that my own body won’t let me keep up the pace I need to maintain.
Tell me about the role your partner, family, or close friends played during your pregnancy. What was their involvement like?

Honestly, my role as "solo" as I call it, means their involvement is mostly… at arm's length. My friends are supportive in the way that they text me to ask how I’m feeling, and they’ll offer to grab me a coffee or whatever, but they don't really *know*. They don't know the late-night panic or the way I stare at my bank statements. I think they’re scared of saying the wrong thing, so they keep it light, which just reinforces that wall I’ve built up.

My family is even further away. I haven’t really told them the extent of my fear because I don’t want them to worry, and I definitely don't want them to think I can't handle this. I want to show up to the birth or the first few months and be the capable, strong person they always knew. But sometimes, when the silence in my place gets really heavy, I wish I could just call my mom and say, "I’m terrified, and I don't know if I’m ready." 

Instead, I keep most of it to myself. I’ve realized that I’m the only one who can truly be there for this pregnancy. It’s isolating, but in a strange way, it’s also given me this weird, quiet confidence that I didn't have before. I’m doing it, even if I’m doing it alone.
Tell me about any worries or fears you carried during your pregnancy — about your health, your baby, the birth, or anything else.

The biggest fear isn't actually the labor—though that's scary enough on its own. It's the moments *after*. I spend so many nights staring at the ceiling, thinking about that first week home alone. Who do I call if I’m exhausted beyond belief, or if I just need to hear that I’m doing a good job? When you don't have a partner, the "what-ifs" feel ten times bigger. What if I’m not enough? What if the baby is crying and I’m so sleep-deprived that I don't know what to do?

I also carry this constant, low-grade anxiety about my health because I know how much the baby depends on me staying "low-risk." Every time I feel a cramp, I’m convinced it’s a sign that I’ve pushed myself too hard at work. I worry that my stress is seeping into the baby, that my uncertainty is somehow bad for their development. 

It’s just… it’s a lot of weight for one person to carry. I’m doing the research, I’m following the vaccine schedules, I’m being the "perfect" patient, but that’s all clinical. The real fear is the loneliness. It’s not just about surviving the pregnancy; it’s about figuring out how to be a mother when the rest of the world seems to have a partner to lean on.
Were you able to share those fears with your care team — and if so, how did they respond?

To be honest, I haven't really brought those fears up to them. Every time I’m in the office, I feel like there’s this invisible clock ticking, and the midwife has to get to her next appointment. I’m just one of many, a routine check-in. If I started talking about the loneliness or the panic about being a single mom, it would feel like I was veering off-script. I’m afraid they’d just see me as "overwhelmed" or "anxious" instead of just being a normal person who’s doing something really hard.

I worry that if I say, "I’m terrified of being alone," they’ll put a note in my file that labels me differently. I want to be the "healthy, low-risk" patient because that feels like the safest place to be. If I act like everything is fine—if I just focus on the Tdap schedule and the blood pressure—they treat me with efficiency and respect. If I show them the messy, scared part of myself, I’m not sure they’d know how to handle it. So, I keep it inside. I treat my medical care like a transaction, and I save all the "real" stuff for the 3:00 AM hours in my apartment. It’s lonely, but it keeps the clinical relationship clean.

When you first started thinking about having a child, what did you hope your relationship with your healthcare providers would look like — and were there any ways you worried it might not live up to that?

I think I hoped for a partnership. I wanted that classic, supportive midwife relationship you read about—someone who would remember my name, someone who would sit with me and actually *listen* to the fears I have about doing this solo. I wanted someone who would look at me not as a collection of stats, but as a woman embarking on the biggest, scariest chapter of her life. I guess I wanted to feel like I had a teammate in all of this.

But I think I knew, deep down, that the reality would be much more fragmented. My biggest worry was that I’d be nothing more than a chart number to them. And honestly? That’s exactly what it feels like. I worry that if I come in with a list of emotional questions, they’ll just look at me like I’m an inconvenience. 

I’m terrified that when I go in for my appointments, they’re just waiting for me to hit the milestones so they can move on to the next patient. It’s hard to build a relationship when you’re just a time slot on a busy Tuesday afternoon. I’m doing my best to be the "perfect" patient, but I can’t help feeling that my need for a real connection is just going to be ignored.

Thinking back to when you and your partner — or the people closest to you — were weighing up whether and when to start trying for a baby, how did those conversations actually go? Who felt like they had the most say, and did that feel right to you?

Since I am going through this on my own, there wasn't a "we" to have those conversations with. That’s probably the most honest thing I can tell you. When I realized I was pregnant, there was no deliberation, no pros-and-cons list shared over a kitchen table, no negotiation about timing. It was just... me, standing alone in my apartment, coming to terms with the fact that the decision had been made for me by life itself.

Looking back, I think I always had this secret, quiet hope that one day I’d have that kind of partnership—where you sit down and say, "Okay, are we ready? Can we afford this? Is this the right time for our careers?" But because that wasn't my reality, I didn't get to have any of those moments. I didn't have anyone to lean on, and I didn't have anyone to share the power with. 

Sometimes I feel a bit of bitterness about that, especially when I see other women who have someone to hold their hand through the uncertainty. But I’m also realizing that because I didn't have a partner, the "say" was entirely mine. That’s a heavy, lonely kind of authority, but it’s mine. I didn’t get the conversation, but I am living the outcome. I’m just trying to make sure I’m the best version of myself for this baby.
Did older family members — parents or grandparents — have a view that found its way into the decision, even indirectly?

My mother has always been vocal about her life, and while she never sat me down and gave me a lecture on when to have children, the expectations were always just... hanging in the air. She grew up in a generation where you followed a very specific script: you get married, you have the house, and then you have the baby. Because I’ve strayed so far from that—being twenty-seven, unmarried, and doing this solo—I feel like there's this silent, invisible judgment hanging over me whenever I think about her perspective.

Even though she’s never said it, I feel like she thinks I’m doing it the "hard way." She looks at my life and sees a lack of stability because there’s no man in the picture to provide the foundation. It’s indirect, but it colors everything. I find myself wanting to prove her wrong, to show her that I can be both the provider and the nurturer, even without that "traditional" setup. 

It’s exhausting, really. You’re trying to deal with the physical reality of growing a human, and at the same time, you’re trying to silence the voices of your family in your head. I’m doing this my way, but I’d be lying if I said I didn't feel the weight of their old-fashioned ideas on my shoulders every single day.
Before you became pregnant, how did you feel about your body and what it was capable of — and did those feelings change at all as you started thinking about pregnancy?

Before I was pregnant, I mostly saw my body as a tool—something that had to be strong enough to get me through a ten-hour shift, fit enough to carry my groceries up the stairs, and thin enough to fit society's standard of "okay." I treated it like a piece of equipment I was responsible for maintaining. I was pretty critical of it, actually. I’d look in the mirror and nitpick things that didn’t really matter, constantly pushing it to do more than it probably wanted to.

When I started thinking about pregnancy, that shifted into something almost protective, yet terrifying. I suddenly realized that this "equipment" was going to be the literal vessel for another life. It wasn't just about my own convenience anymore. I felt this intense pressure for my body to be "good enough," healthy enough, and resilient enough to handle something as monumental as growing a baby. 

Now that I’m actually pregnant, it’s like my relationship with my body is in a total state of flux. I’m in awe of it for doing all this, but I’m also so scared that I’ll break it—or that it’ll fail me. It’s strange to be so grateful for the heartbeat I felt, yet so tired from the physical drain. I’m learning, slowly, that my body is capable of more than just "work." It’s capable of life. It’s a terrifying, powerful shift.

If you saw a GP, nurse, or any other health professional before you became pregnant — even just for something routine — what was that experience like, and did it give you any sense of what maternity care might be like down the line?

I didn’t really go in for "routine" check-ups before this. Like a lot of people my age who are just grinding through the work week, I only went if something was actually broken or bothering me. Even then, it felt very transactional—a quick physical, a prescription, and out the door.

That experience actually makes me nervous about maternity care. It makes me feel like my health is just a box to be checked. When you’re used to being in and out of an office in ten minutes, it’s hard to suddenly believe that these same people are going to take the time to really listen to my fears or help me navigate the "solo" part of this journey. 

I’ve had this sense that the healthcare system is designed for people who have someone else in the room to help them remember things or ask the hard questions. Since I’m alone, I feel like I’m at a disadvantage. I’m just waiting for the day where they start rushing me or acting like my questions about my own well-being are an imposition. It feels less like a partnership and more like a series of appointments where I have to prove I’m doing everything "right" just to get the care I need. It’s hard to trust a system that’s never felt like it was really looking out for *me* as a person.
Before you were pregnant, what kinds of conversations were you having with the people around you — family, friends, your partner — about having a baby, and whose input felt most meaningful to you?

Honestly, before this happened, those conversations were mostly hypothetical or just jokes with friends about how "not ready" we were. We’d be out on a Friday, tired from our shifts, and someone would say, "Can you imagine adding a baby to this mess?" and we’d all laugh because it sounded like a disaster. I didn’t have serious, deep-dive conversations with anyone because, truthfully, I didn't think it was on my horizon.

My friends were focused on their careers or their own relationships, and I was just trying to stabilize my own life here in Fall River. If I had talked about it seriously, I think the response would have been, "Dian, you’re doing this on your own—are you sure you want that weight?" So, I kept it all internal. I didn't want the lecture or the "maybe you should wait" talk. 

The only input that ever really carried any weight was my own internal monologue. I had this idea of being a mother one day, but it was always pushed to some distant, "when I have it all figured out" future. Now that it’s here, I realize that "figuring it out" was just a story I told myself to feel safe. Looking back, I wish I’d talked to someone who didn't just judge the circumstances, but someone who could have told me it’s okay to be scared. But that person didn't exist in my world then.
Did your mum, mother-in-law, or older women in your family share their own experiences — and did that help or complicate things?

My mom’s stories are a mixed bag. When I was younger, she’d talk about birth as this "heroic" thing women just *did*—like it was this final exam you passed to become a "real" adult. She’d gloss over the hard parts, the exhaustion, the nights where she felt like she was drowning in diapers and crying. It made motherhood seem like this monolith I had to conquer, rather than a messy, human experience.

It complicates things because when I’m struggling now, I feel like I’m failing the "heroic" standard she set. If I mention that I’m overwhelmed or that I don't have a plan for every single scenario, I feel like I’m letting her down, or like I’m admitting I’m not as strong as she was. It makes me feel like I have to perform being "put together." 

I really wish there had been more honesty about the grit—not just the sacrifice, but the fear. Instead, I’m left trying to navigate my own pregnancy while wondering why I don't feel that same effortless, "it-will-all-work-out" ease she seems to project. It’s hard to tell her how much I’m struggling because I’m afraid she’ll just see it as a sign that I shouldn't be doing this alone. I just keep quiet and try to forge my own, very different path.

Were there things about your own body, your health history, or just the way you felt about yourself physically that made the idea of pregnancy feel complicated or uncertain — even before anything had actually happened?

Before I was pregnant, I definitely had this complex relationship with my body. I spent so many years seeing it as something that needed to be "managed"—a series of workouts to balance out my desk job, or a set of dietary rules to keep myself feeling okay. I think I felt a lot of pressure, just from being in my twenties, to have a body that was "ready" for whatever life threw at it, but "ready" usually meant "looking a certain way."

The idea of being pregnant felt so uncertain because I was terrified of losing that control. I worried that if my body changed—if it didn't just snap back or if it developed complications—I wouldn't know how to handle the version of myself that wasn't "on" or "fit." I felt like I had to prove I was capable of doing this, and that meant having a body that was perfectly aligned with what I thought a healthy pregnancy looked like.

It’s ironic, because now that I’m actually growing a life, those old insecurities seem so shallow. But they haven't disappeared. Sometimes I still catch myself worrying about the physical toll, wondering if I'm "enough" for this, or if my body is going to be able to keep up. It’s hard to let go of that critical voice, especially when I’m navigating all this by myself. I’m still learning that my body is so much more than a project to be managed—it’s an anchor.

In the time before you became pregnant, was there any one person — professional or otherwise — who felt like a consistent, reliable source of support or guidance, someone you could come back to with questions?

That’s the hardest part, really. Looking back, there wasn't anyone who fit that role—no mentor, no steady family confidant, and definitely no doctor who really *knew* me. I’ve always operated as a solo entity, just navigating the tides on my own. I think I built this wall because I thought if I didn't let anyone in, I couldn't be disappointed by them not being there. 

When I look at my life now, I see that I’ve been my own only source of reliable guidance for a long time. It’s lonely, but it’s been effective. I research, I plan, I strategize—it’s how I survive. But there’s a difference between "surviving" and having someone to lean on. I find myself wishing I had that one person—a teacher, an aunt, a doctor—who would have just looked at me and said, "It’s okay not to have the answers right now."

Instead, I feel like I’m constantly looking for that person in the clinical system, but they’re just too busy. Every appointment is with someone new, or someone who is checking boxes. I feel like a sailor without a map, just trying to read the stars, hoping I don't hit the rocks. If I had that one person, maybe the silence in my apartment wouldn't feel so heavy.
What made that person feel reliable or trustworthy as a source of support at that stage?

It’s almost impossible to answer that, because that person simply didn't exist for me. If I had to imagine what that person *would* have felt like—what would have made them reliable—it wouldn't be about how many degrees they had or how much technical advice they could rattle off. It would be about their presence. It would be someone who could sit in the silence with me without rushing to fill it with "shoulds" or "to-do" lists.

I think reliability, to me, means someone who doesn't look at their watch when you're talking. Someone who wouldn't just see me as a "low-risk case" or a "single woman in Fall River," but as a whole person with fears that go way deeper than a vaccine schedule. Trustworthiness, in this state, feels like being heard without being judged for being scared.

If I had found that in a doctor or a mentor, I think I would have felt safe enough to let my guard down. I would have been able to stop performing the "perfect patient" role and just be me—the one who’s worried about money, the one who’s lonely at night, the one who’s doing her best. But since I didn't have that, I’ve had to be my own anchor. It’s reliable, I guess, but it’s a very solitary kind of trust.

As you were thinking about whether to have a baby, did it feel like a decision you were genuinely free to make in your own time and your own way — or were there pressures, expectations, or circumstances that meant it was never quite fully your choice?

That’s a heavy question to sit with. If I’m being honest, it never felt like a “decision” at all. It felt more like a collision. There was no serene moment where I sat down, weighed my life, and decided it was time. Life just… intervened.

When you’re twenty-seven and working full-time in Fall River, trying to keep a roof over your head and your head above water, you feel like you’re constantly running a race. Adding a baby into that mix wasn’t part of the roadmap I’d drawn for myself. I feel like I’ve been pushed into this role of "motherhood" before I ever felt ready to step into it. 

People talk about "choice," but when you’re facing the reality of an unexpected pregnancy, the choices feel narrow and the pressure feels immense. There was the pressure to be the person who “has it together,” the pressure to not be a burden, and the internal, crushing pressure to be perfect because I knew I’d be doing it solo. It wasn't my choice in the way it’s portrayed in those romanticized stories. It’s a reality I’m still learning to own. It feels like I’m running a race I didn't sign up for, in shoes that don't quite fit, and I’m just trying to make sure I don't stumble.
Were there financial, relationship, or cultural factors that made you feel like the timing was out of your hands?

The financial reality is the biggest thing. When you live paycheck to paycheck, you don't really have the luxury of "timing." You just exist in a state where you're constantly trying to avoid the next catastrophe. When the test came back positive, I didn't get to look at my savings and feel confident; I looked at my bank balance and felt fear. I wasn't at a place in my career where I could just take maternity leave without worrying about whether my job would be there when I wanted to come back. That isn't a choice; that's just survival.

And then there's the cultural weight—that silent, heavy pressure of being unmarried. I feel like I’m constantly being measured against a standard I never signed up for. I didn’t choose to be a single mother; that was the hand I was dealt, but the world treats it like a deficit. I feel like I have to work twice as hard to prove that I’m "capable" because I don’t have a partner beside me to validate my life choices.

It wasn't that the timing was out of my hands—it’s that my hands were already so full with just trying to live. I feel like I was pushed onto a stage before I’d even finished reading the script. I’m doing my best, but I’m doing it from a place of being reactive, not proactive.
Once you were pregnant, how much did you feel like you were in the driving seat of your own care — and were there moments when you felt yourself handing that over to others, willingly or not?

I wish I could say I felt like I was in the driver's seat, but most days it feels like I’m in the backseat, watching a stranger steer the car. From the moment I stepped into that clinic, it was like a set of rails clicked into place. They have their protocols, their schedules, and their checklists. When I’m there, I feel like I have to defer to them because they’re the "experts" and I’m just the person who hasn't done this before. 

I’ve definitely handed over the reins, and it wasn’t entirely willing. It’s more that I don't feel I have the authority to challenge the way things are done. If I ask a question that's too "unusual" or "emotional," I can feel the dismissiveness. So, I stop asking. I let them measure me, weigh me, and scan me, and then I walk out. 

The hardest part is that I *want* to be in the driver’s seat. I want to feel like I’m part of the team, not just a passenger. But in a system that feels so clinical and impersonal, handing over the responsibility feels like the only way to get through without feeling like I’m constantly being judged. It’s a strange paradox: I’m the one who will be raising this child, but in those clinic rooms, I feel like I’m just waiting for permission to be a mother.
Were there points where relying on others felt like a relief, and others where it felt like losing something?

It’s a complicated trade-off. There are days when I am so exhausted—when the nausea is relentless and the stress of my work feels like it might snap me in two—that I *want* to be able to just hand it all over. In those moments, when I’m sitting in the clinic and the midwife tells me, "Just do this, follow this schedule, take these," it’s a relief. It’s like, *okay, I don’t have to figure this one piece out by myself right now.* It’s a temporary pause in the weight of being the sole person responsible for everything.

But then, that relief curdles. It feels like I’m losing my voice in the process. When I stop questioning things because I’m afraid of being labeled the "difficult" patient, I feel like I’m losing a piece of myself. I’m the one who will be looking into this baby’s eyes at 3:00 AM, and yet, the people who are currently directing my care don't seem to care about *my* voice, just the data points they’re collecting. 

It feels like every time I let them take control to get some "relief," I’m losing the confidence I need to be the mother I want to be. I’m becoming a spectator in my own pregnancy, and that’s a terrifying loss. It’s a very fragile balance, and I haven't quite figured out how to stay in the driver’s seat while still accepting help.

When decisions came up during your pregnancy — about tests, appointments, birth plans, or anything else — how did you and your partner navigate those together, and did it ever feel like you weren't quite on the same page?

Since there’s no partner in the room, those "decisions" are essentially just me, sitting at my kitchen table, talking to myself. I don’t have someone to turn to when I’m staring at a brochure for prenatal testing, wondering if the extra cost is worth the peace of mind. I don’t have a partner to discuss birth plans with, to practice breathing exercises, or to help me weigh the pros and cons of a hospital vs. a birthing center.

Sometimes, when I'm at an appointment and the midwife asks, "What do you think about this?" I feel a sharp pang of loneliness. I realize that I’m the only one who has to hold the weight of these choices. It never feels like I'm "on the same page" with anyone else because I'm reading from a book I'm writing in real-time, by myself. 

If I had a partner, maybe we could have argued or debated, but at least we would have been *two* sets of hands. Now, it’s just my hands, shaking a little as I sign the consent forms. I have to be the one to decide, the one to worry, and the one to live with the outcome. It’s not that I’m "not on the same page" as anyone—it’s that I’m standing in a room by myself, trying to figure out how to fill all the space.
Were there decisions where your partner's view really mattered to you, and ones where you needed it to be your call alone?

It’s hard to even picture that kind of dynamic, to be honest. I’ve spent so long being the only person responsible for my life that the idea of a partner having a "view" that I’d genuinely weigh against my own feels like a luxury I never had. 

If I *had* had someone, I think I would have desperately wanted their input on the big stuff—like what kind of birth experience I should aim for or how we’d balance the first few weeks at home. Just having someone to say, "Hey, we’ve got this," would have changed everything. It would have felt like the pressure was halved.

But then, there are these deep, internal things—like how I feel about my body, or the specific way I’m trying to handle the prenatal screening—that feel like they belong only to me. Even with a partner, I think I would have felt the need to make those calls on my own, just to prove I could. I’ve had to become so self-reliant that my default is to hold everything inside. I’m so used to being the only one in the room that even if someone *was* there, I’d probably still be the one making the final decision in my own head. It’s a habit I’m not sure I know how to break.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I guess... if there’s one thing I really want people to understand, it’s that being "low-risk" is a clinical label, not an emotional one. Everyone seems so focused on the physical safety—the heart rate, the weight, the blood pressure—and that’s important, truly. But there’s a whole other layer of "risk" that nobody tracks. There’s the risk of feeling like you’re doing it all in a vacuum, the risk of the silence in the apartment becoming louder than the doctors, and the risk of feeling like you’re losing yourself in the process of becoming a mother.

I want the system to see that a first-time mother, especially one on her own, is carrying a weight that doesn't show up on a lab report. I’m not just a set of charts. I’m a person who is trying to be brave enough to hold this life, even when I’m terrified that I’m not enough. 

It would have meant everything if just one person had stopped the clock during an appointment, looked me in the eye, and asked, "How are *you* doing, really?" Not "how is the baby," but *me*. I think that simple acknowledgment—that I am seen as a person, not just a procedure—would have changed the entire tone of this journey for me. Thank you for listening. It actually feels a little lighter just to say that out loud.
